name: Integrals — Undoing the Derivative and Adding Up
whatIsIt: Integration is the REVERSE of differentiation. The indefinite integral ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (add one to the power, divide by the new power, add a constant C). A DEFINITE integral ∫ from a to b gives a single number — the total, found by substituting the top and bottom limits and subtracting.
whyNeeded: Integration adds up infinitely many tiny pieces — distance from speed, total charge from current, area under a curve, volume of a shape. It's the other half of calculus, essential across physics and engineering.
realLifeUses: Total distance travelled from a speed graph
Total water flow from a changing rate
Work done by a varying force

vocabulary.meaning: The reverse of the derivative; also a way to add up tiny pieces.
∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (add one to the power, divide by it).
An unknown constant added to every indefinite integral (its derivative is 0).
∫ from a to b — substitute both limits and subtract to get a number.

story.title: Distance from Speed
story.text: Tara knows a scooter's speed changes, and asks how far it went. Sir says: 'Speed is the derivative of distance, so distance is the INTEGRAL of speed — integration undoes it.' For a speed of 2x, distance = ∫2x dx = x² + C. Over the interval 0 to 3, that is [x²] from 0 to 3 = 9 − 0 = 9 metres. 'Integration added up all the little bits of distance,' Tara smiles.
story.extractedProblem: Evaluate the definite integral ∫ from 0 to 3 of 2x dx.
story.answerInStory: 9 — ∫2x dx = x², and x² from 0 to 3 is 9 − 0 = 9.

visual.caption: The shaded area under y = 2x from x = 0 to 3 is the definite integral ∫₀³ 2x dx = 9. Integration adds up the region under the curve.

teachingGallery.title: Add one to the power
teachingGallery.note: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C — the opposite of the derivative's 'bring down and drop'.

examples.caption: ∫x dx: power 1 → 2, divide by 2 → x²/2 + C.
∫x² dx: power 2 → 3, divide by 3 → x³/3 + C.

standardMethod.summary: Integrate xⁿ by adding one to the power and dividing by the new power, plus C. For a definite integral, drop C, substitute the top and bottom limits, and subtract.
steps: For ∫xⁿ dx: new power is n+1; divide by n+1 and add C.
Check by differentiating — you should get back the original.
For a definite integral, find the antiderivative (no C needed).
Substitute the upper limit, then the lower limit.

workedExamples.problem: Evaluate ∫ from 0 to 2 of x dx.

commonMistakes.mistake: Leaving out the + C on an indefinite integral.
commonMistakes.fix: Every indefinite integral needs + C, since the derivative of any constant is 0. ∫x dx = x²/2 + C.

commonMistakes.mistake: Bringing the power DOWN (like differentiating) instead of adding one to it.
commonMistakes.fix: Integration ADDS one to the power and divides: ∫x² dx = x³/3 + C, not 2x.

easy.q: In ∫x² dx = x³/3 + C, what is the new power of x?

challenge.q: A student writes ∫x² dx = 2x. What's the fix?

options.label: Add one to the power: x³/3 + C

challenge.answer: Add one to the power: x³/3 + C
hintLadder: Integration is the reverse of differentiation.
Add one to the power, divide.

questions.q: New power of x in ∫x³ dx = x⁴/4 + C?

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up that ∫x dx = x²/2 + C reverses the derivative (differentiate it back to check). Then evaluate ∫ from 0 to 3 of 2x dx = 9 by substituting the limits into x².

revisionCard.summary: Integration reverses differentiation. ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (add one to the power, divide, +C). Definite ∫ₐᵇ: substitute top and bottom limits and subtract (no C). E.g. ∫₀³ 2x dx = [x²]₀³ = 9. Don't bring the power down — ADD one.

trickPractice.intro: Antiderivative, substitute the limits, subtract. Fast rounds of ∫ₐᵇ.

questions.q: New power of x in ∫x⁴ dx?

rememberIt.hook: Integration = reverse of differentiation = area under the curve
rememberIt.unpack: integral x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (add one to the power, divide by it). Always +C for indefinite integrals.

studentQuestions.q: How is it the 'opposite' of a derivative?
studentQuestions.a: Differentiating x^n gives n x^(n-1); integrating undoes that - add one to the power and divide by the new power.
Constants vanish when you differentiate, so going back you cannot know the constant - you add C to cover every possibility.

formulas.name: Power rule (integral)
formulas.remember: Add one to the power, divide by it (n != -1)

formulas.name: Definite integral
formulas.formula: integral a..b f dx = F(b) - F(a)
formulas.remember: Top value minus bottom value = area
